channel.title: Posts on Asgar
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on Asgar

item.title: Zero-Trust Hardening a Proxmox Hypervisor to a Single UDP Port
item.description:
Overview

A default Proxmox VE installation exposes four services on the public internet. The Proxmox web UI (port 8006), SSH (port 22), rpcbind (port 111), and the SPICE proxy (port 3128) are all reachable from anywhere on the internet with no network-layer protection. This post documents a hardening approach that reduces the public attack surface to a single UDP port using Tailscale as a zero-trust access layer and iptables to enforce the network boundary at the kernel level.

item.title: Detecting Post-Phishing Inbox Rule Abuse in Microsoft 365
item.description:
Detection Context

This post documents a Sigma rule contributed to SigmaHQ targeting post-phishing inbox rule abuse in Microsoft 365 Exchange Online.

Log Source: M365 Unified Audit Log
Technique: MITRE ATT&CK T1114.003 — Email Collection: Email Forwarding Rule
Status: Experimental — pending production validation

The Technique

After a successful phishing attack, credential theft alone is rarely the end goal. Persistent access to a victim mailbox is more valuable — and inbox rules are how attackers get it.

item.title: Identifying Geolocation of Attacker using Wireshark
item.description:
Forensic Context

This walkthrough uses the WebStrike scenario from CyberDefenders.

Artifact: PCAP file containing network traffic
Goal: Identify the attacker’s geographic location

Note: To learn how to acquire PCAP files, see DocuSign’s documentation on How to do a Packet Capture (PCAP).

First, establish the victim IP. You can identify your own IP using:

curl ifconfig.me

Or via What Is My IP.

Finding the Attacker IP

With the victim IP established, identify network conversations in Wireshark: Statistics → Conversations.
